findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that the cleanup script must implement its own 429/5xx retry-with-Retry-After wrapper around each DELETE call (the existing `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` / `_direct_rest_delete` path does NOT retry HTTP 429 — it re-raises immediately); update the rate-limiting mechanism section to reflect this as new script-level work.
evidence: Plan: 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After'
acli_rest.py:62-64: 'except urllib.error.HTTPError: # HTTP errors (4xx/5xx) are deterministic — do not retry. raise'
_direct_rest_delete calls _rest_urlopen_with_retry (acli_rest.py:319) which will immediately raise on HTTP 429
Retry-After handling lives only in acli_subprocess.py (for ACLI CLI subprocess stderr parsing), not in the REST DELETE path
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After' for the DELETE loop, implying the existing `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` / `_direct_rest_delete` infrastructure provides this. However, `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` (acli_rest.py:62-64) explicitly re-raises `urllib.error.HTTPError` immediately without retrying — meaning HTTP 429 and 5xx responses from DELETE calls are NOT retried by the existing code. The cleanup script must implement its own 429/Retry-After retry wrapper around DELETEs; the plan does not acknowledge this gap, leaving the AC 'Rate-limit safety: DELETE loop throttles and retries 429/5xx with backoff' relying on infrastructure that does not deliver it.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Rate limiting section; AC 'Rate-limit safety'
scenarios: Cleanup script reuses delete_comment/acli_graph which calls _direct_rest_delete -> _rest_urlopen_with_retry; a 429 from Jira propagates as an unhandled exception, aborting the deletion run mid-stream rather than backing off.

The AC unit test 'asserts a 429 is retried (not dropped) and Retry-After is honored' would need to mock a layer the current code does not provide, silently validating against a stub rather than real behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' section, change the description from implying reuse of existing infrastructure to: 'The cleanup script wraps each DELETE in its own retry loop: catch `urllib.error.HTTPError` with code 429, read `Retry-After` header, sleep accordingly, then retry (cap at N attempts); also retry on 5xx. This cannot reuse `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` (which re-raises all HTTPError immediately).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Pagination mechanism how the cleanup script obtains Jira's reported `total` for the completeness check (e.g. a separate REST GET on the issue's comment field, or a modified paginator that captures the `total` from the first page JSON) — the existing `_parse_paginated_comments` discards the `total` field and cannot support this guard as-written.
evidence: Plan: 'the script reconciles fetched-count against Jira's reported total per ticket and REFUSES to proceed if they differ (guards a silently-truncated fetch)'
Plan: 'Reuse the live-validated acli.py::get_comments(key) with --paginate + _parse_paginated_comments'
acli_cli_ops.py:516-543: _parse_paginated_comments returns list[dict[str, Any]] — no total field propagated to caller
_parse_acli_comments (acli_cli_ops.py:495-513) also returns only a list; the 'total' key from each page JSON object is silently dropped
findings.finding: The plan claims the script 'reconciles fetched-count against Jira's reported total per ticket and REFUSES to proceed if they differ', describing this as using the live-validated `acli.py::get_comments` + `_parse_paginated_comments`. However, `_parse_paginated_comments` returns only a flat `list[dict]` with no 'total' metadata — the Jira-reported total (the `total` field in the paginated JSON pages) is discarded during parsing. Obtaining the reported total requires either separately fetching it (e.g. via a single non-paginated REST call) or modifying the parser — neither of which is described. The plan presents the completeness check as already-supported by reuse, when it is actually new work.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Pagination section; AC backup completeness check
scenarios: Script calls get_comments(), gets a list of N dicts, but has no Jira-reported total to compare against — the completeness guard as described cannot be implemented by reusing the existing function without modification or supplemental REST call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `jira_reported_total` is obtained for the completeness assertion — either by extending `_parse_paginated_comments` to return `(comments, total)`, adding a separate REST call to fetch issue comment metadata, or documenting that `total` is read from the last page's envelope (requiring a custom parse loop).
evidence: Plan: 'the script reconciles fetched-count against Jira's reported `total` per ticket and REFUSES to proceed if they differ'
acli_cli_ops.py:495-513: `_parse_acli_comments` extracts only the `comments` array from each page dict, discarding `total`, `isLast`, `startAt`
acli_cli_ops.py:532-543: `_parse_paginated_comments` calls `_parse_acli_comments(obj)` and concatenates — no `total` surface
Neither `AcliClient.get_comments` nor `acli_cli_ops.get_comments` returns a tuple with `total`
findings.finding: The plan's per-ticket completeness assertion (`len(backup[key]) == jira_reported_total`) requires Jira's reported `total` field, but `_parse_paginated_comments` (the named reuse target) discards the ACLI response envelope entirely — it returns only the flat comment list and does not expose `total`. There is no named mechanism in the plan to obtain the `total` count for the assertion.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Pagination; Pre-deletion backup AC bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC1
scenarios: Cleanup script calls `get_comments(key)` to build the backup, gets back a list of N dicts, but has no way to compare against Jira's reported total without an additional call or a modified parse — the completeness guard cannot be implemented as described using only the named reuse target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the DELETE loop routes through (or reimplements) 429+Retry-After retry logic — `_direct_rest_delete` / `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` explicitly does NOT retry on HTTPError, so the plan's '429 retry honoring Retry-After' claim requires either wrapping `delete_comment` in `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` or adding explicit 429 handling in the cleanup script itself.
evidence: Plan: 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After'
acli_rest.py:62-64: 'except urllib.error.HTTPError: # HTTP errors (4xx/5xx) are deterministic — do not retry. raise'
dispatch_one.py:142-151: _call_with_retry handles urllib.error.HTTPError 429 with Retry-After, but _direct_rest_delete does not route through it
acli_graph.py:546-554: delete_comment calls _direct_rest_delete directly
findings.finding: The plan claims 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After' for the DELETE loop, but the existing `_direct_rest_delete` (the natural reuse vehicle for `delete_comment`) calls `_rest_urlopen_with_retry`, which explicitly does NOT retry on HTTPError — '4xx/5xx are deterministic — do not retry' (acli_rest.py:62-64). HTTP 429s from Jira will be raised immediately as uncaught exceptions, not retried. The 429-aware retry with Retry-After lives in `dispatch_one._call_with_retry`, which is NOT on the `_direct_rest_delete` call path.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — Rate limiting + transient-failure handling / src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_rest.py:36-83
scenarios: At ~10 req/s over ~64k DELETEs, Jira Cloud will almost certainly issue 429s; these will crash the script mid-run rather than being retried, requiring manual restarts and making the 'resumable' property load-bearing but untested
A 429 during a batch of 1000 deletes mid-ticket leaves the ticket partially cleaned — resumable by rerun, but only if the script catches HTTPError 429 explicitly
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Rate limiting' mechanism section, add: 'Each DELETE call is wrapped with `dispatch_one._call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, comment_id)` (which already handles HTTPError 429 + Retry-After and 5xx backoff) rather than calling `client.delete_comment` directly; the ≥100ms inter-call sleep is applied between successful DELETEs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete retry mechanism for DELETE calls (e.g. wrapping `client.delete_comment` in `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` or a new equivalent), since `_direct_rest_delete` / `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` do NOT retry on HTTP 429/5xx.
evidence: Plan: 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After'
acli_rest.py:62-64: 'except urllib.error.HTTPError: # HTTP errors (4xx/5xx) are deterministic — do not retry. raise'
`delete_comment` at acli_graph.py:546 calls `self._direct_rest_delete(path)` which calls `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` — no 429 retry there
429-aware retry lives in `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py:104), not named in the plan
findings.finding: The plan states the DELETE loop will 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After', but the existing `_direct_rest_delete` → `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` path explicitly does NOT retry on `HTTPError` (acli_rest.py:62-64: 'HTTP errors (4xx/5xx) are deterministic — do not retry'). The 429/5xx retry with Retry-After logic lives in `dispatch_one._call_with_retry`, which is not named as a reuse target for the DELETE path. If the cleanup script calls `client.delete_comment(...)` directly, 429s will raise rather than retry.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Rate limiting + transient-failure handling; AC: Rate-limit safety
scenarios: Operator runs --execute; Jira returns 429 mid-run; `delete_comment` raises HTTPError immediately without retry; the deletion loop aborts or skips comments silently, violating the stated safety guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each `client.delete_comment(key, cid)` call in `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, cid)` (importing from `dispatch_one`) so 429/5xx retry + Retry-After honoring is inherited from the already-tested path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the cleanup script implements its OWN 429/5xx retry loop with Retry-After handling around each DELETE call (it cannot inherit this from `_direct_rest_delete` / `_rest_urlopen_with_retry`, which re-raise all HTTPError immediately); update the plan to make this explicit rather than implying the existing REST transport covers it.
evidence: acli_rest.py:62-64: `except urllib.error.HTTPError: # HTTP errors (4xx/5xx) are deterministic — do not retry. raise` — _rest_urlopen_with_retry passes 429 straight through as an exception.
acli_rest.py:304-319: _direct_rest_delete routes through _rest_urlopen_with_retry with no additional retry wrapper.
429/Retry-After handling exists only in dispatch_one.py (reconciler mutation dispatcher) and acli_subprocess.py (ACLI subprocess layer) — neither is on the cleanup script's call path.

Plan states 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After' without clarifying this requires new code, not reuse of existing infra — a capability assertion that is unverified as stated.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the DELETE loop will 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After' but the existing `_direct_rest_delete` transport (`_rest_urlopen_with_retry`) explicitly re-raises all `urllib.error.HTTPError` immediately without retry — 429 and Retry-After handling is NOT inherited from the existing REST infra.
findings.location: Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section; AC line: 'Rate-limit safety: DELETE loop throttles and retries 429/5xx with backoff'
scenarios: Cleanup script calls delete_comment() for one of ~64,000 DELETEs; Jira returns HTTP 429; _rest_urlopen_with_retry re-raises immediately; script crashes or skips the comment rather than backing off — the run is neither resumable gracefully nor rate-limit-safe.

An operator reads the plan expecting the existing AcliClient infrastructure to handle 429 and does not implement a retry wrapper in the cleanup script, leaving the run vulnerable to rate-limit failures across 13 × ~4900 DELETEs.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Rate limiting' section, add: 'NOTE: `_direct_rest_delete` / `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` do NOT retry HTTPError — the cleanup script must wrap each `delete_comment()` call in its own retry loop that catches `urllib.error.HTTPError` with code 429 or ≥500, sleeps for `Retry-After` seconds (or exponential backoff), and retries up to N times before failing the individual deletion and logging it.' Reference `dispatch_one.py:138-155` as the pattern to replicate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the cleanup script must implement its own per-DELETE 429/5xx retry-with-backoff loop (wrapping `client.delete_comment()`), since `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` explicitly does NOT retry HTTP 4xx/5xx errors — and update the AC unit-test description to reference the script-level retry wrapper, not the transport.
evidence: Plan: 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After'
acli_rest.py:62-64: 'except urllib.error.HTTPError: # HTTP errors (4xx/5xx) are deterministic — do not retry. raise'
acli_graph.py:546-554: delete_comment() calls _direct_rest_delete() which calls _rest_urlopen_with_retry()
acli_subprocess.py:111-144: 429-backoff exists only for the _run_acli subprocess path, not REST DELETE
Evidence class: Contradicted — the environment's existing REST transport falsifies the plan's retry claim for the DELETE path
findings.finding: The plan asserts the DELETE loop will 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After', but the existing REST transport the cleanup script would reuse — `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` in `acli_rest.py` — explicitly does NOT retry on HTTP 4xx/5xx (including 429): it raises `urllib.error.HTTPError` immediately on any HTTP-level error. The 429-backoff logic in `acli_subprocess.py` applies only to the ACLI subprocess path, not to `_direct_rest_delete`. A cleanup script calling `client.delete_comment()` in a loop gets zero 429-retry from the existing infrastructure, contradicting the plan's assertion and its own AC ('unit test asserts a 429 is retried').
findings.location: Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section; AC bullet 4 ('Rate-limit safety: DELETE loop throttles and retries 429/5xx'); acli_rest.py:62-64
scenarios: At ~64k+ DELETEs across 13 tickets, Jira Cloud rate-limits the script; without 429-retry the script crashes mid-run with an unhandled HTTPError, leaving the ticket set in a partially-cleaned state and requiring manual triage of the deletion log to determine what was completed.

The AC unit test for '429 is retried' cannot pass against the current `delete_comment` implementation without additional wrapper logic — the test would either test non-existent behaviour or require the cleanup script to add its own retry layer that the plan doesn't acknowledge as a new component.
findings.suggested_fix: In the script's DELETE loop, catch `urllib.error.HTTPError`, check `exc.code in (429, 500, 502, 503, 504)`, parse `Retry-After` from `exc.headers`, sleep, and retry — mirroring the logic in `acli_subprocess._rate_limit_backoff`. Alternatively, add a `delete_comment_with_retry()` helper in the cleanup script that wraps `client.delete_comment()` with this policy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement a 429/5xx retry layer for the DELETE loop that is NOT delegated to _rest_urlopen_with_retry (which explicitly does not retry HTTPError): either wrap _direct_rest_delete with a separate 429-aware retry decorator, or introduce a new _direct_rest_delete_with_rate_limit_retry that reads Retry-After and sleeps before re-issuing the DELETE request.
evidence: Plan states: 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After'
acli_rest.py:62-64: 'except urllib.error.HTTPError: # HTTP errors (4xx/5xx) are deterministic — do not retry. raise'
acli_rest.py docstring: 'Does NOT retry on urllib.error.HTTPError (4xx / 5xx) — HTTP-level error semantics are unchanged.'
_direct_rest_delete (acli_rest.py:319) delegates to _rest_urlopen_with_retry — inheriting its no-HTTP-retry behavior
Jira Cloud 429 responses arrive as HTTPError, so the existing transport will raise immediately — contradicting the plan's stated safety guarantee
findings.finding: The plan's DELETE-loop retry protocol ("retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After") is specified as a concrete mechanism but the named reuse vehicle — _direct_rest_delete → _rest_urlopen_with_retry — explicitly does NOT retry on HTTP errors: the code reads 'Does NOT retry on urllib.error.HTTPError (4xx / 5xx) — HTTP-level error semantics are unchanged. Raises the original exception after all attempts are exhausted.' A 429 from Jira will immediately propagate as an unhandled HTTPError, aborting the DELETE loop rather than backing off. The plan asserts a retry protocol that does not exist in the infrastructure it names for reuse.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' section; acli_rest.py:36-83 (_rest_urlopen_with_retry), acli_rest.py:304-320 (_direct_rest_delete)
scenarios: Executor reuses _direct_rest_delete for the ~64k DELETEs; Jira rate-limits at 429; the exception propagates uncaught, aborting mid-run with a partial delete list and no retry — the opposite of the plan's stated behavior.
Unit test AC4 ('a unit test asserts a 429 is retried') will fail RED against the existing _rest_urlopen_with_retry because it raises immediately on 429.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a thin retry wrapper around each DELETE call in the cleanup script: catch `urllib.error.HTTPError` where `e.code in (429, 500, 502, 503, 504)`, read `e.headers.get('Retry-After', default_backoff)`, sleep, and retry up to N times — independent of the existing `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` which is not designed to handle this case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the retry-exhaustion policy (e.g., max N attempts then abort the full run with non-zero exit and a summary of failed comment IDs, OR log as 'FAILED' outcome and continue) and ensure the deletion log uses distinct outcome values for 404-success vs. retry-exhausted-failure.
evidence: 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After' — no max-retry count or exhaustion behavior defined
Deletion log records 'outcome' per comment, but the plan does not specify what outcome value is written or what control flow follows a final failure
findings.finding: The plan specifies retry on HTTP 429/5xx but does not define the behavior when retries are exhausted (e.g., after max attempts: abort the entire run, skip the comment and continue, or halt the ticket's remaining deletions). Without a specified policy, the script may silently swallow the failure and produce a partially-cleaned ticket with no operator signal distinguishing 'intentionally kept' from 'failed to delete'.
findings.location: Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section; AC: 'Rate-limit safety' bullet
scenarios: Jira experiences a prolonged 5xx outage mid-run; retries exhaust; the script either silently continues (leaving un-deleted duplicates with no clear log signal) or crashes with no clean exit, leaving the append-only log in an ambiguous state.
Operator re-inspects the deletion log and cannot distinguish 'skipped because already deleted (404)' from 'skipped because retries exhausted (5xx)'.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a max_retries constant (e.g., 5) and on exhaustion: log the comment id with outcome='FAILED_EXHAUSTED', increment a failure counter, and after the full run exit non-zero with a summary line 'N deletions failed after retries — re-run --execute to resume'. This keeps the run resumable while surfacing failures clearly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback state assertion: specify the reconciler's expected behavior on the next pass after a content-restore (body-equality dedup confirms already-mirrored vs. re-adds), and confirm whether any local Jira-comment-ID state must be cleared or refreshed after restore.
evidence: 're-POST its body to the ticket (note: re-created comments get NEW ids/timestamps — content-restore, not id-restore)'
Plan does not state the reconciler's behavior on the next pass after restore
Plan does not state whether any local datastore mapping Jira comment IDs needs to be invalidated or refreshed post-restore
findings.finding: The rollback/restore path ('re-POST its body to the ticket') does not assert the post-restore state of the system. T11 requires the plan to assert the schema/data STATE after rollback, not merely describe the restore mechanism. Specifically: (1) re-POSTed comments get new IDs and timestamps — the plan is silent on whether the rebar local state or any stored Jira-comment-ID mapping remains consistent; (2) re-POSTed bot comments will carry the RECONCILER_MARKER (since the backup body includes it), but the plan doesn't confirm whether the reconciler will treat them as already-mirrored or attempt to add them again on the next pass.
scenarios: After content-restore, the reconciler runs and uses body-equality dedup — if the restored body already matches a local comment, it would correctly skip re-adding. But if the backup body contains the RECONCILER_MARKER and the reconciler strips the marker before comparing, the match would succeed. Unverified without explicit assertion in the plan.
If rebar locally caches Jira comment IDs (not confirmed either way), stale IDs post-restore would cause the reconciler to mis-identify comments on the next diff pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the deletion log artifact path in AC6 (e.g. `artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletion-log-<ts>.jsonl`) and add a proving command such as `jq -e 'select(.outcome)' artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletion-log-<ts>.jsonl | wc -l` to verify it is non-empty and structured correctly.

ABSENCE: no file path, no `ls`/`jq` proving command for AC6
findings.finding: The deletion log artifact (AC6) has no specified file path, making its proving command incomplete — an executor cannot verify 'the deletion log artifact exists' without knowing where to look.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 ('The deletion log artifact... exists for the executed run.')
scenarios: An executor completes the run but cannot verify AC6 because the log file could be at any path — or silently omitted — and pass the criterion by omission.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC6: 'Log path: `artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletion-log-<ts>.jsonl`. Proving command: `jq -e length artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletion-log-<ts>.jsonl >/dev/null && wc -l artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletion-log-<ts>.jsonl` (must be > 0 for an execute run).'

AC4: 'a unit test asserts a 429 is retried (not dropped) and Retry-After is honored.' — no test file path or pytest command

findings.finding: AC3 and AC4 each reference unit tests that must pass (or go RED for the negative case) but neither provides a concrete proving command (e.g. a `pytest` invocation with a module path), leaving an executor without an in-session check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('A unit test over a synthetic comment set asserts...') and AC4 ('a unit test asserts a 429 is retried...')
scenarios: An executor writes the script but skips the unit tests, or writes them in the wrong location; without a proving command the AC cannot be checked in CI or in-session.
The CI run cannot be cited as evidence without a named test module/path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the DELETE loop wraps `client.delete_comment(...)` in the existing `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (or equivalent re-export) rather than re-implementing 429/5xx/Retry-After retry from scratch; cite where `_call_with_retry` is used if already intended.
evidence: Plan: 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After'

dispatch_one.py:104: `_call_with_retry` — 'Retryable: TimeoutError; JiraAPIError 5xx/429; and urllib.error.HTTPError 5xx/429 … 429 honors a present integer Retry-After, else ADR-0036 jittered backoff'
dispatch_one.py:142-150: raw `urllib.error.HTTPError` from `_direct_rest_delete` is explicitly caught and 429/5xx retried
_errors.py:32: `parse_retry_after` — already the canonical Retry-After parser
ABSENCE: plan never mentions `_call_with_retry` or `parse_retry_after` as the vehicle for this retry logic
findings.finding: The plan re-specifies a custom '429/5xx retry with exponential backoff honoring Retry-After' for the DELETE loop, but `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py:104–165) already implements exactly this: 429 and 5xx are retryable, `Retry-After` is parsed via `parse_retry_after`, ADR-0036 jittered backoff applies, and 404 is treated as success. Re-describing the same logic without referencing `_call_with_retry` risks a divergent or incomplete re-implementation.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' section
scenarios: If the cleanup script's DELETE loop re-implements retry from scratch, it may omit edge cases already handled (e.g. raw HTTPError vs JiraAPIError distinction, backoff cap at MAX_BACKOFF_S=60s, 5xx vs 429 branching) that _call_with_retry gets right.
A divergent retry implementation could also miss the 404-as-success path that the plan's own idempotency guarantee relies on.
findings.suggested_fix: In the cleanup script, call `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, comment_id)` (importing from `dispatch_one`) for each deletion. This automatically gets 429/5xx retry, Retry-After honor, ADR-0036 jitter, and 404-as-success handling — and the proactive 100ms inter-call sleep can sit outside this call to handle the throttling concern separately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit concurrency note specifying whether the reconciler should be paused during the --execute phase (or document that concurrent operation is safe/accepted with the stated self-healing behavior), and confirm the keep-oldest selection is made from the same snapshot used to build the delete list within a single run.
evidence: 'run ONLY after P1 is live' ensures no new duplicates from P1 forward, but the reconciler continues to run on its normal cron schedule during the cleanup window
The keep-one grouping is 'recomputed from the live paginated set each run' (re-run safety) but within a single --execute run the fetch is a snapshot, not a live view during deletes
Absence of any mention of pausing the reconciler or of explicit concurrency analysis between the cleanup script and the live reconciler
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the reconciler should be paused (or its run frequency acknowledged as a concurrent actor) during the delete phase. A TOCTOU window exists: the script fetches all comments, identifies the keep-oldest set, then issues ~64k DELETEs over several minutes. If the reconciler runs during that window and successfully posts a new copy of a body that was in the fetched set, the cleanup script may delete what was the only surviving copy of that body in its local view, leaving Jira with one copy (the reconciler's new one) — benign in isolation — but this is not acknowledged and the interaction is unspecified.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms; Sequencing safety check (T4 safety plan)
scenarios: Cleanup fetches 5000 comments, identifies body B's oldest copy as comment X (keep), Y and Z as deletes. Reconciler runs, sees B is already mirrored (X exists), adds nothing. Cleanup deletes Y and Z. Result: X alone, correct.

Cleanup fetches 5000 comments; reconciler runs BEFORE cleanup deletes X (the only copy at that moment due to a prior partial run), sees B is already present, adds nothing; cleanup then deletes X (was marked as a duplicate in the stale fetch). Result: body B disappears from Jira. Reconciler will re-add it on next run — self-healing but not acknowledged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the reconciler should be paused/quiesced for the duration of `--execute`, or document the re-inflation risk explicitly and confirm the resumable re-run path is sufficient mitigation.
evidence: Throttle to ≤10 req/s → ~64k DELETEs ≈ 107 minutes of execution during which reconcile passes can fire
Plan confirms 'reconcile-bridge run 29625135085 on commit a6cdad0e2 = green' — reconciler continues running on its normal schedule
No instruction to pause or quiesce the reconciler during the execute window
findings.finding: The plan does not address concurrent reconciler activity during the ~107-minute execute window. New bot-authored comments posted by the reconciler AFTER the initial paginated fetch (but before execution completes) are invisible to the delete sweep and may create fresh duplicates that are only cleaned up on the next re-run. More critically, the plan does not specify whether the reconciler should be quiesced during execution; without that, a reconciler pass could observe a transient zero-copy state for a given body mid-sweep and re-add the comment, partially re-inflating a ticket.
findings.location: Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section; 'Idempotent + resumable' section
scenarios: Reconciler runs mid-sweep, sees a body temporarily at zero Jira copies (cleanup just deleted the kept oldest copy and hasn't finished), and re-adds it — creating a new duplicate that must be cleaned on a follow-up run.

A fresh bot comment posted during execution is not in the fetched paginated list and persists correctly, but the accounting (fetched-count == jira_reported_total) assertion done at start-of-run may no longer hold at end-of-run, confusing the operator.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-execute step that pauses the reconciler schedule (or documents a maintenance window), then re-enables it after the run. Alternatively, add an explicit note that re-inflation during execution is expected, bounded, and handled by a follow-up `--dry-run` + `--execute` pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test that exercises both abort paths: (a) simulate a paginated fetch returning fewer comments than Jira's reported `total` and assert the script refuses to proceed (no DELETEs fired), and (b) simulate a backup where `len(backup[key]) != jira_reported_total` and assert deletion is refused — both should be RED if the guard is missing or off-by-one.
evidence: 'the script asserts, per ticket, len(backup[key]) == jira_reported_total ... deletion is refused if the assertion fails'
'the script reconciles fetched-count against Jira's reported total per ticket and REFUSES to proceed if they differ'
Neither of the two named unit tests in the AC covers these abort branches.
findings.finding: The two named unit tests cover the deletion filter and 429-retry logic, but the plan's safety-critical abort paths — (1) backup completeness assertion fails (fetched count ≠ Jira-reported total) → refuse deletion, and (2) paginated fetch count mismatch → refuse — are new conditional logic branches with no specified unit test. These are explicitly called out as guards against dangerous partial-delete scenarios, yet no AC line names a test that fires them.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC bullet 1 (backup completeness check) and 'Concrete mechanisms' → Pagination section ('REFUSES to proceed if they differ')
scenarios: A silently truncated paginated fetch returns 4900/5000 comments; without a test, the abort guard could be incorrectly implemented (e.g., off-by-one, wrong field) and the script would delete based on an incomplete list — exactly the dangerous scenario the plan warns against.
A backup write failure (partial JSON) passes the count check due to a bug; no test exercises the refusal path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC bullet: 'A unit test asserts that when `fetched_count != jira_reported_total` (simulated truncated fetch), the script raises/exits before any DELETE is issued; and when the backup count assertion fails, the script similarly refuses. Both tests confirm zero DELETE calls were made.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the authentication mechanism for DELETE calls (e.g. 'reuse the same `JIRA_API_TOKEN` / `JIRA_EMAIL` env-vars that `acli.py` uses') so an executor can configure the script without a clarifying question.
evidence: Plan reuses `acli.py::get_comments` for reads but specifies only 'DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}' for writes — no auth reference
ABSENCE: no mention of which credential/env-var the DELETE calls use
findings.finding: The plan does not state the authentication mechanism for the DELETE `/rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}` calls — whether the script reuses `acli.py`'s auth (env-var token, OAuth, service-account key) or requires separate credentials configuration is not specified, which could block an executor setting up the script.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' / 'delete via DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}'
scenarios: An executor scaffolding the delete script does not know whether to pass the same ACLI_TOKEN env-var, a different service-account credential, or an OAuth header — leading to a 401 that halts the run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Rate limiting' mechanism section: 'Auth: reuse the same env-vars (`JIRA_API_TOKEN`, `JIRA_EMAIL`) that `acli.py` already reads; no additional credential setup required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete restore procedure to the rollback section: a `--restore` mode (or separate restore script) that reads `backup-<ts>.json` and re-POSTs each specified comment body via `POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment`, with a dry-run option, and include this as an AC.
evidence: 'if a comment must be recovered, re-POST its body to the ticket (note: re-created comments get NEW ids/timestamps — content-restore, not id-restore)'
The Acceptance Criteria contain no AC for a verified or tested restore path.
ABSENCE: no restore script, curl invocation, or step-by-step procedure is specified for recovering from an accidental over-deletion.
findings.finding: The rollback path for deleted comments is described only as 're-POST its body to the ticket' with no concrete restore procedure — no script, no command, no step-by-step instructions. T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not merely an acknowledgement that content can be re-posted.
scenarios: A subset of comments is incorrectly identified as duplicates (e.g. a normalization edge-case) and deleted. The operator needs to restore them but has no concrete procedure — they must hand-craft Jira REST calls from raw JSON, under pressure, with no validated tooling.
A partial run deletes comments on 5 of 13 tickets before failing. Operator needs to restore selectively but the backup JSON has no accompanying restore command.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--restore <backup-file> [--ticket REB-XXX]` flag to the cleanup script that reads the backup JSON and re-POSTs comment bodies for specified tickets (or all 13). Include an AC: 'A restore dry-run over the backup artifact completes without error and lists the comments it would re-create.' This makes the rollback mechanism concrete and tested, not just described.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a per-request socket/read timeout (e.g., 30 s) for all HTTP calls (DELETE and paginated GET), distinct from the retry-backoff logic, so a hung connection surfaces as a timeout error that feeds into the existing retry/exhaustion path.
evidence: 'retry on HTTP 429 / 5xx with exponential backoff' — covers HTTP-level errors but not socket/connection-level hangs
Absence of any per-request timeout specification in the plan
findings.finding: No per-request timeout is specified for individual DELETE (or paginated GET) calls. A TCP-level hang (Jira accepts the connection but never responds — not a 429 or 5xx) would stall the script indefinitely with no recovery path, violating the resumability guarantee.
scenarios: Jira's API gateway accepts the TCP connection for a DELETE but hangs (e.g., during a partial Jira outage). The script blocks forever on that request, the append-only log has the delete as in-flight, and the operator must manually kill the process.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass `timeout=30` (or similar) to every `requests` call and treat `requests.exceptions.Timeout` as a retryable transient failure alongside 5xx, feeding it into the existing backoff loop.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
